channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Mideast Is Baffled by Trump’s Call to Expand Abraham Accords
item.description: The president said more countries should be required to recognize Israel as part of a deal to end the war with Iran. Analysts say the chances of that happening are close to zero.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim, Isabel Kershner and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with leaders from Israel, Bahrain and the United Arab Emirates at the signing of the Abraham Accords in Washington in 2020.

item.title: ‘Both Parties Kind of Get It Wrong’: The Young Men Who May Swing the Midterms
item.description: Many Gen Z men who voted for Donald Trump are dismayed by his time in office. But they say they are not hearing an appealing pitch from Democrats, either.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Emma Goldberg
item.media:credit: Anna Watts for The New York Times

item.title: James Talarico Opens Texas Senate Campaign Against Ken Paxton With Houston Rally
item.description: Personal attacks and a packed campaign rally set the tone for what is likely to be a rancorous contest in Texas for U.S. Senate between James Talarico and Ken Paxton.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Representative James Talarico campaigned in Houston on Wednesday.

item.title: Judge Declines, for Now, to Block Mail-In Voting Changes Ordered by Trump
item.description: The ruling allowed the Trump administration to continue to pursue changes that include enlisting the Postal Service in checking voters against a national database.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A voter casting a mail-in ballot in June.

item.title: Michigan Governor Gretchen Whitmer Will Not Run for President in 2028
item.description: Governor Whitmer of Michigan had been viewed as part of a field of possible Democratic candidates. She has been governor for two terms, and is barred from running again.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan at an event in Detroit in April.

item.title: In Argentina, U.S. Tech Billionaire Peter Thiel Finds An Escape
item.description: The billionaire’s new roots in Argentina are said to be partly motivated by concerns about the future of the United States and shared beliefs with Argentina’s right-wing leader.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Peter Thiel, right, arriving for a meeting with President Javier Milei at the Casa Rosada Presidential Palace in Buenos Aires in April.

item.title: I Tried to Sell My House With A.I.
item.description: Over five frantic days, I gambled my family’s life savings on a hunch that A.I. could outperform a real estate agent.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson

item.title: Watchdog Agency Seeks to Reverse Its Win Against Firm Run by Winklevoss Twins
item.description: The Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed jointly with a company run by the Winklevoss twins to overturn their settlement.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere
item.media:description: Gemini’s founders, Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, are among the president’s closest allies in the crypto industry.

item.title: Dell Gets a $9.7 Billion Defense Contract. Trump’s Portfolio Stands to Benefit.
item.description: The president’s family says his stock purchases are automatically timed, but recent acquisitions of the computer company’s shares raise questions.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton
item.media:description: Michael and Susan Dell pledged in December to donate $6.25 billion to kick off the “Trump accounts” program that creates special investment savings accounts with tax benefits for American children.

item.title: Bari Weiss Names Nick Bilton to Lead CBS’s ’60 Minutes’ in Major Shake-Up
item.description: Bari Weiss, CBS’s editor in chief, named Nick Bilton, a tech journalist and filmmaker, as the show’s executive producer. The network also fired two on-air correspondents.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: Nick Bilton is a filmmaker and former New York Times columnist.

item.title: Trump Refiles $10 Billion Lawsuit Against The Wall Street Journal
item.description: An earlier version of the defamation suit, which focused on an article about a birthday note to Jeffrey Epstein, was dismissed by a judge.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Juan Arredondo for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s lawsuit centered on a Wall Street Journal story published in July 2025.

item.title: Two Papers Named ‘Star’ Will Duel in D.C. to Win Readers From The Washington Post
item.description: First, The Star was born. Now, The Washington Star is reborn. Both are hoping to win readers ceded by recent layoffs at The Washington Post.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: The Washington Star, previously known as The Washington Star-News, stopped publishing in 1981.

item.title: ‘Vibrant as Ever’: Harvard’s Jewish Life Thrives as Trump Investigates
item.description: Trump officials have focused on episodes during protests over the war in Gaza at Harvard and other schools. Jews on campus describe a changed atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Mark Arsenault
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: At Harvard University, students and others say the culture is different from the one depicted in the Trump administration’s lawsuit.

item.title: Anthropic Tops OpenAI to Become the World’s Most Valuable A.I. Start-Up
item.description: Anthropic raised $65 billion in new fund-raising that put its value at $900 billion, ahead of OpenAI’s last valuation of $730 billion, as the companies duel for A.I. dominance.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic’s updated model, Claude Opus 4.8, is particularly adept at vibe coding, or the process of artificial intelligence writing code from prompts in conversational English.

item.title: What’s Inside New York’s $269 Billion Budget: Second-Home Tax, Restrictions on ICE and More
item.description: Gov. Kathy Hochul used the state budget as a vehicle for policy initiatives, including a new second-home tax and a push to make the state less hospitable to immigration agents.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Kathy Hochul insisted on making climate goals easier to reach as part of the budget deal.

item.title: Mamdani Vowed to Back Espaillat. Now He May Endorse a D.S.A. Challenger.
item.description: The mayor is seriously considering getting behind Darializa Avila Chevalier, a democratic socialist challenging Representative Adriano Espaillat in an Upper Manhattan House primary.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Maya King
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times; Heather Diehl/Getty Images

item.title: Paul McCartney on His New Album and the Beatles’ Earliest Days
item.description: On his new album, the ex-Beatle revisits his past and revels in simply continuing to create.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Paul McCartney performing in London in 2024.

item.title: Greenpeace’s Long War With a Pipeline Titan Enters a Strange New Phase
item.description: This month, a North Dakota court barred Greenpeace from saying what it wanted in a European court, an unusual move. The environmental group says it is forging ahead.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: A court building in Amsterdam. The North Dakota Supreme Court this month barred Greenpeace International from making claims against Energy Transfer in a Dutch countersuit.

item.title: Happy, Bronx Zoo Elephant at Center of Animal Rights Case, Is Dead at 55
item.description: Activists had sued in a bid to secure her the fundamental human right to bodily liberty. Zoo officials said she was well cared for and called the lawsuit frivolous.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.media:description: Happy the elephant had lived at the Bronx Zoo since 1977.

item.title: Lizzo Addresses the Backlash and Performs Live at The New York Times
item.description: During a special edition of “Popcast,” the singer discussed her new album and the public fallout from a series of lawsuits. Then she performed a stripped-down set.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli and Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Three Ways Trump Is Losing the War in Iran
item.description: Three reasons Trump is losing the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: David French
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Our Military Is Built for the Wrong Century
item.description: Ukraine and Iran have shown us that war as we’ve known it is over.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Victoria Chamberlin
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Everlane Co-Founder, Shocked by Shein Acquisition, Wants a Redo
item.description: Michael Preysman, who ran the brand for a decade, can’t believe what happened to the company he built. Can he start over?
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:description: Michael Preysman, a co-founder of Everlane, is among a cohort of founders who have bemoaned the fate of their brands after giving up control.

item.title: Man Sentenced to 40 Years in Murders of 4 Sleeping Homeless Men
item.description: The judge who sentenced Randy Rodriguez Santos said his case exemplified the “coming together of three horrible symptoms of this city: homelessness, mental illness and narcotics abuse.”
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Rashid Umar Abbasi
item.media:description: Randy Rodriguez Santos, center, was sentenced to at least 40 years in prison for the fatal beatings of four men in 2019.

item.title: Supreme Court Says Death Row Inmate Can Challenge Exclusion of Black Jurors
item.description: Terry Pitchford was convicted in 2006 for his role in the murder of a shopkeeper by a 12-member jury that included one Black member.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: The Supreme Court decision means that Mr. Pitchford, 40, who has served death row for more than 20 years, is entitled to a new trial.

item.title: Russ Hodge Dies at 86; an Olympian, Like His Mother
item.description: Nicknamed Russell the Muscle, he finished ninth in the decathlon at the 1964 Games. His mother, Alice Arden, placed the same as a high jumper in 1936.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
